children.name: Caecilia
children.description: The largest and most speciose caecilian genus, comprising legless, soil-burrowing amphibians of Central and South American tropics. Most species are viviparous or direct-developing, secretive inhabitants of forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Caecilia thompsoni
children.description: The longest caecilian at up to 1.5 m; powerful burrower of Colombian rainforest; hundreds of annuli (body rings).

children.name: Caecilia tentaculata
children.description: A robustly built caecilian of northern South America, widespread in Suriname, Guyana and Brazil. A soil burrower of lowland rainforest with numerous annuli and small tentacles near the nostrils.

children.name: Caecilia nigricans
children.description: A large dark caecilian of Colombia and Ecuador, reaching over a meter in length. A powerful soil burrower of lowland and foothill forests.

children.name: Caecilia orozcoi
children.description: A Colombian caecilian known from Andean foothill forests, distinguished by its annulation pattern. Poorly known and rarely encountered.

children.name: Caecilia subnigricans
children.description: A burrowing caecilian of northern South American forests, similar to but smaller than the black caecilian, with a darkish body.

children.name: Caecilia occidentalis
children.description: A burrowing caecilian of the Pacific lowlands of Colombia and Ecuador, inhabiting moist forest soils.

children.name: Caecilia abitaguae
children.description: A caecilian known from the Abitagua region of eastern Ecuador, a burrowing species of humid Amazonian foothill forests. Recognized by subtle annulation differences from related Caecilia species.

children.name: Caecilia albiventris
children.description: A medium-sized burrowing caecilian of northern South America distinguished by pale ventral coloration. Inhabits lowland rainforest soils and is rarely observed above ground.

children.name: Caecilia antioquiaensis
children.description: Endemic to the Antioquia department of Colombia, this caecilian inhabits the soils of Andean foothills. It is poorly studied and known from very few specimens.

children.name: Caecilia armata
children.description: A robustly annulated caecilian of the Amazon basin, named for features of its skull morphology. Burrows in alluvial soils near streams and forest rivers.

children.name: Caecilia augustus
children.description: One of the larger Caecilia species, found in the tropical lowlands of Ecuador and Peru. A burrowing caecilian of humid forest soils with a cylindrical, heavily annulated body.

children.name: Caecilia bokermanni
children.description: A Brazilian caecilian known from the Atlantic Forest biome, named in honor of the herpetologist Werner Bokermann. Lives in moist soils at forest edges.

children.name: Caecilia caribea
children.description: A caecilian of Caribbean-facing lowland forests in Colombia and Venezuela. Burrows in moist soils of humid rainforest and cacao plantations.

children.name: Caecilia corpulenta
children.description: A stout-bodied caecilian of South American tropical forests, with a relatively wide girth for the genus. Inhabits deep, moist soils of the Amazon and Orinoco basins.

children.name: Caecilia degenerata
children.description: A caecilian named for highly reduced, degenerative eyes indicating extreme adaptation to subterranean life. Known from Amazonian Peru and Ecuador.

children.name: Caecilia dunni
children.description: A caecilian of Panama and northwestern Colombia named after the herpetologist Emmett Reid Dunn. Inhabits moist lowland forest soils and is poorly known.

children.name: Caecilia flavopunctata
children.description: A caecilian of the Amazon basin with yellow spotting along its body, unusual among the often uniform-colored Caecilia. A soil-burrowing species of lowland tropical forest.

children.name: Caecilia gracilis
children.description: One of the more slender Caecilia species, found in the tropical forests of northeastern South America. Named for its gracile body proportions relative to other members of the genus.

children.name: Caecilia guntheri
children.description: A caecilian of northern South America named after the herpetologist Albert Günther. A soil-burrowing species of humid lowland forests.

children.name: Caecilia inca
children.description: A caecilian known from the Andean cloud forests of Peru, named for the Inca civilization of the region. Inhabits high-altitude moist soils unusual for the genus.

children.name: Caecilia leucocephala
children.description: A caecilian distinguished by pale head coloration contrasting with the darker body, known from northwestern South America. A burrower of lowland and foothill forest soils.

children.name: Caecilia marcusi
children.description: A Brazilian forest caecilian named after Eveline Marcus, a zoologist who contributed to Brazilian invertebrate and amphibian research. A secretive burrower rarely encountered alive.

children.name: Caecilia pachynema
children.description: A caecilian of the Amazon basin with notably thick body annuli, inhabiting the moist alluvial soils of lowland tropical forest. Rarely observed and known from few specimens.

children.name: Caecilia perdita
children.description: A caecilian from Colombia whose name reflects the difficulty of locating it in the field. Known from only a handful of specimens collected from forest soil.

children.name: Caecilia pressula
children.description: A caecilian of the humid lowland forests of Ecuador and Colombia, named for features of its skull structure. Burrows in moist tropical soils.

children.name: Caecilia subdermalis
children.description: A caecilian of the western Amazon basin with particularly scale-rich skin, the scales being entirely subdermal and revealed only in cross-section. An elusive burrower of forest soils.

children.name: Caecilia tenuissima
children.description: One of the most slender members of the genus, known from the tropical lowlands of Colombia and Venezuela. A burrower of moist alluvial soils.

children.name: Caecilia volcani
children.description: A caecilian of the volcanic highlands of western Panama, one of the few Caecilia found in Central America. Inhabits the moist soils of highland forests on the slopes of ancient volcanoes.

children.name: Caecilia youngorum
children.description: A caecilian of the Amazonian lowlands, named in honor of the Young family who contributed to herpetological collections in South America. A secretive soil burrower.

children.name: Caecilia taquiremboensis
children.description: A caecilian known from Brazil and named after the Taquirembo locality. It inhabits the moist soils of gallery forests and humid lowland areas.

children.name: Caecilia crassisquama
children.description: A caecilian of northern South America with notably robust subdermal scales. Known from forested areas of Colombia and Venezuela, where it burrows in moist tropical soils.

children.name: Caecilia disossea
children.description: A medium-sized caecilian of Central American lowlands, named for features of its reduced skull ossification. Inhabits moist soils in humid tropical forests.

children.name: Caecilia isthmica
children.description: A caecilian of the Central American isthmus, found in lowland and foothill forests of Costa Rica and Panama. One of the few Caecilia species extending into Central America.

children.name: Typhlonectes
children.description: Fully aquatic caecilians with laterally compressed tails, live-bearing and adapted to permanent life in rivers and streams of South America.

children.name: Typhlonectes compressicauda
children.description: Fully aquatic; laterally compressed tail for swimming; bears live young; widely kept in aquaria and popular in research on limbless locomotion.

children.name: Typhlonectes natans
children.description: A fully aquatic live-bearing caecilian of Colombian river systems, particularly the Cauca River basin. Swims with sinuous body movements and gives birth to aquatic young.

children.name: Typhlonectes cunhai
children.description: An aquatic caecilian of South American rivers and swamps, fully adapted to life in water with a finned tail region. Gives live birth to larvae provisioned in the oviduct.

children.name: Ichthyophis
children.description: Asian caecilians with parental care.

children.name: Ichthyophis glutinosus
children.description: Lays eggs in moist burrows; female coils around clutch for months until hatching; one of the most studied oviparous caecilians.

children.name: Ichthyophis bombayensis
children.description: A striped caecilian of the Western Ghats near Mumbai (Bombay), India. Egg-laying with maternal guarding; inhabits moist forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Ichthyophis kohtaoensis
children.description: A striped caecilian of Thailand, Laos and Cambodia, often found in rice paddies and stream banks. Females coil around their eggs until they hatch into aquatic larvae.

children.name: Ichthyophis bannanicus
children.description: The only caecilian native to China, found in Yunnan and Guangxi as well as Vietnam. A striped, egg-laying caecilian of moist lowland habitats.

children.name: Ichthyophis beddomei
children.description: A striped caecilian of the Western Ghats of India, inhabiting moist forest soils and plantations. Females guard their egg clutches.

children.name: Ichthyophis tricolor
children.description: A striped caecilian endemic to the southern Western Ghats of India, named for its tricolored appearance. Lives in moist soil and leaf litter.

children.name: Ichthyophis malabarensis
children.description: A caecilian of the Malabar region of the Western Ghats, India. A soil-dwelling, egg-laying species of forested hill habitats.

children.name: Ichthyophis orthoplicatus
children.description: A striped caecilian endemic to the wet zone and hills of Sri Lanka, found in moist soils of forests and tea plantations.

children.name: Ichthyophis humphreyi
children.description: A Southeast Asian striped caecilian of forest soils, an egg-laying species with aquatic larvae.

children.name: Siphonops
children.description: Skin-feeding caecilians of South America whose hatchlings feed on the mother's specially thickened outer skin — a remarkable form of parental care known as dermatophagy.

children.name: Siphonops annulatus
children.description: Young scrape and eat layers of the mother's outer skin as their first food — a form of skin-feeding parental care described only in 2006; the mother's skin doubles in thickness in preparation.

children.name: Siphonops paulensis
children.description: A ringed caecilian of southern Brazil, Paraguay, Bolivia and northern Argentina. Like its relatives, mothers feed their young on a specialized layer of skin (dermatophagy).

children.name: Siphonops hardyi
children.description: A small ringed caecilian of southeastern Brazil's Atlantic Forest. Practices maternal skin-feeding of the young.

children.name: Siphonops insulanus
children.description: A ringed caecilian of Brazil, distinguished from the common ringed caecilian by subtle annulation differences; a soil dweller with maternal skin-feeding.

children.name: Siphonops leucoderus
children.description: A caecilian of northern Brazil with notably pale inter-annular grooves giving a banded appearance. Like all Siphonops, the young feed on their mother's thickened skin shortly after hatching.

children.name: Siphonops polyzona
children.description: Named for its many body rings, this caecilian occurs in humid forests of central and northern South America. A secretive burrower with maternal skin-feeding of hatchlings.

children.name: Siphonops rostratus
children.description: Named for its projecting rostral region, this caecilian inhabits the Amazonian and Cerrado soils of Brazil. It shares the skin-feeding behavior characteristic of the genus.

children.name: Dermophis
children.description: Central American caecilians, viviparous (live-bearing) burrowers of soil and leaf litter. The Mexican caecilian is the best-studied member and a model for caecilian reproduction.

children.name: Dermophis mexicanus
children.description: A live-bearing caecilian of Mexico and Central America that gestates young internally, feeding fetuses on uterine secretions. Found in agricultural soils and forests; listed as Vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Dermophis oaxacae
children.description: A burrowing caecilian of southwestern Mexico, named for the state of Oaxaca. A live-bearing species of moist lowland and montane soils.

children.name: Dermophis glandulosus
children.description: A live-bearing caecilian of Costa Rica, Panama and Colombia, with conspicuous skin glands. Found in lowland and foothill forest soils.

children.name: Dermophis costariquensis
children.description: A live-bearing caecilian endemic to Costa Rica, inhabiting moist Caribbean-slope forests and banana plantations. Gives birth to fully formed young fed in utero on uterine secretions.

children.name: Gymnopis
children.description: Central American viviparous caecilians lacking eye openings as adults, burrowing in forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Gymnopis multiplicata
children.description: A live-bearing caecilian of Central America with a purplish-grey body and numerous body rings. Common in moist soils of forests, gardens and banana plantations from Honduras to Panama.

children.name: Geotrypetes
children.description: African soil caecilians, viviparous burrowers of West and Central African forests. The genus has become important in laboratory research on caecilian biology.

children.name: Geotrypetes seraphini
children.description: A live-bearing West and Central African caecilian, one of the few caecilians kept and bred in the lab. Pinkish-grey and soil-dwelling, it gives birth to fully developed young.

children.name: Geotrypetes pseudoangolensis
children.description: A viviparous soil caecilian of West and Central Africa, originally confused with an Angolan species, giving rise to its name. Inhabits moist forest soils and is live-bearing like all Geotrypetes.

children.name: Geotrypetes angeli
children.description: A caecilian of West Africa named after the herpetologist F. Angel, found in humid forest soils of Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana. A live-bearing burrower.

children.name: Schistometopum
children.description: Brightly colored African caecilians, including vivid yellow species of coastal East Africa and São Tomé. Their striking coloration is unusual among the typically drab caecilians.

children.name: Schistometopum thomense
children.description: A brilliant yellow caecilian endemic to the island of São Tomé in the Gulf of Guinea. Locally abundant in soils and plantations; one of the most colorful caecilians.

children.name: Schistometopum gregorii
children.description: A yellow-bodied caecilian of coastal Kenya, Tanzania and Mozambique, found in moist soils near rivers and floodplains.

children.name: Schistometopum ephele
children.description: A yellow caecilian of coastal Tanzania with fine darker mottling on its skin, distinguishing it from its plain-colored relatives. Inhabits moist soils in coastal forest and scrub.

children.name: Boulengerula
children.description: East African soil caecilians famous for maternal skin-feeding (dermatophagy): hatchlings peel and eat the lipid-rich outer skin of their mother. Named after the herpetologist George Boulenger.

children.name: Boulengerula taitanus
children.description: A Kenyan soil caecilian of the Taita Hills whose hatchlings feed on their mother's specially thickened, nutrient-rich skin, a behavior first documented in this species.

children.name: Boulengerula uluguruensis
children.description: A caecilian endemic to the Uluguru Mountains of Tanzania, restricted to montane forest soils and threatened by deforestation.

children.name: Boulengerula boulengeri
children.description: A soil caecilian of the Eastern Arc Mountains of Tanzania, an egg-laying species restricted to montane forest soils.

children.name: Boulengerula changamwensis
children.description: A caecilian from the Changamwe area near Mombasa, Kenya, inhabiting coastal forest soils. An egg-laying species with maternal skin-feeding of hatchlings.

children.name: Boulengerula fischeri
children.description: A caecilian of the East Usambara Mountains of Tanzania, named after a collector who worked in East Africa. A montane forest soil-dweller threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Boulengerula niedeni
children.description: A caecilian of the Shimba Hills of coastal Kenya, an area of high biodiversity. Inhabits moist forest soils and practices maternal dermatophagy.

children.name: Boulengerula spawlsi
children.description: A recently described Kenyan caecilian named after herpetologist Steve Spawls. Inhabits highland forest soils and is distinguished by subtle morphological features from related species.

children.name: Boulengerula mtotwe
children.description: A caecilian described from coastal Kenya, named using the Swahili word for 'child' in reference to its small size within the genus. An egg-laying burrower of humid coastal forest soils.

children.name: Herpele
children.description: Stout-bodied African forest caecilians of Central and West Africa, egg-laying soil burrowers with maternal skin-feeding behavior.

children.name: Herpele squalostoma
children.description: A robust caecilian of Central and West African rainforests whose young engage in maternal dermatophagy, feeding on the mother's skin. A widespread but secretive soil dweller.

children.name: Herpele multiplicata
children.description: A caecilian of West and Central Africa with numerous body rings (annuli), giving rise to its name. An egg-laying burrower of humid forest soils from Nigeria to the Congo basin.

children.name: Scolecomorphus
children.description: Tropical African caecilians remarkable for tentacles mounted near the nostrils and, in some, eyes carried on the tentacle that can be protruded from the head.

children.name: Scolecomorphus kirkii
children.description: An East African caecilian of Malawi, Tanzania and Mozambique notable for an eye attached to the base of its tentacle, which it can push out of the skull. A live-bearing soil dweller of montane forests.

children.name: Scolecomorphus vittatus
children.description: A striped caecilian of Tanzania's Eastern Arc and coastal forests, with protrusible tentacle-mounted eyes. Restricted to forest soils threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Grandisonia
children.description: Caecilians endemic to the Seychelles islands, an ancient relict radiation isolated since the breakup of Gondwana.

children.name: Grandisonia alternans
children.description: A caecilian endemic to the granitic Seychelles, part of an ancient lineage isolated on the islands for tens of millions of years. Lives in moist forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Hypogeophis
children.description: Seychelles endemic caecilians, egg-laying soil burrowers that form a key part of the islands' relict amphibian fauna.

children.name: Hypogeophis rostratus
children.description: The most widespread Seychelles caecilian, found on several granitic islands. Females lay eggs in burrows and guard them until hatching into miniature adults (direct development).

children.name: Gegeneophis
children.description: Small soil caecilians endemic to peninsular India and the Western Ghats, egg-laying species with maternal care that are still being newly described.

children.name: Gegeneophis ramaswamii
children.description: A small soil caecilian of the southern Western Ghats, India, where mothers brood eggs and the young feed on the mother's skin. A model species for studying caecilian maternal care.

children.name: Gegeneophis carnosus
children.description: A pinkish soil caecilian endemic to the Western Ghats of India, an egg-laying species of moist forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Indotyphlus
children.description: Tiny soil caecilians endemic to the Western Ghats of India, among the smallest and least-known caecilians.

children.name: Indotyphlus battersbyi
children.description: A small caecilian endemic to the northern Western Ghats near Pune, India. Lives in lateritic soils and is rarely seen above ground.

children.name: Uraeotyphlus
children.description: Western Ghats caecilians intermediate between primitive and advanced families, egg-laying with an aquatic larval stage.

children.name: Uraeotyphlus oxyurus
children.description: A reddish-brown caecilian endemic to the Western Ghats of southern India, with a pointed tail. Lays eggs that develop through an aquatic larval stage in streams.

children.name: Rhinatrema
children.description: Primitive South American caecilians with a true tail and aquatic larvae, representing one of the most ancient caecilian lineages.

children.name: Rhinatrema bivittatum
children.description: A small striped caecilian of the Guianas and northern Brazil, one of the most primitive living caecilians, retaining a true tail. Lays eggs that hatch into aquatic larvae.

children.name: Epicrionops
children.description: Andean and Central American caecilians of an ancient family, tailed and egg-laying with free-living larvae.

children.name: Epicrionops bicolor
children.description: A primitive, tailed caecilian of the Andes of Ecuador and Peru, living in cool montane streams and soils. Retains larval features lost in more advanced caecilians.

children.name: Chthonerpeton
children.description: Aquatic and semiaquatic South American caecilians, live-bearing relatives of Typhlonectes found in rivers, swamps and wet soils.

children.name: Chthonerpeton indistinctum
children.description: A semiaquatic, live-bearing caecilian of southern Brazil, Uruguay and Argentina, found in marshes, ditches and slow waters. Gives birth to well-developed young.

children.name: Potomotyphlus
children.description: Fully aquatic South American caecilians of the Amazon basin, with a strongly compressed, eel-like body and finned tail for swimming.

children.name: Potomotyphlus kaupii
children.description: A wholly aquatic caecilian of the Amazon and Orinoco basins, with a laterally flattened, eel-like body adapted to permanent life in rivers. Live-bearing and rarely encountered.

children.name: Microcaecilia
children.description: Small Neotropical soil caecilians of the Guiana Shield and Amazonia, with many recently described species. Among the smallest caecilians, rarely exceeding 20 cm in length.

children.name: Microcaecilia dermatophaga
children.description: A small French Guianan caecilian in which hatchlings peel and consume the lipid-rich outer skin of their mother, the first New World caecilian shown to do so, reflected in its species name.

children.name: Microcaecilia unicolor
children.description: A uniformly colored small caecilian of the Guianas, a secretive soil-dweller of lowland rainforest.

children.name: Microcaecilia albiceps
children.description: A small caecilian of the Guiana Shield distinguished by its pale white head contrasting with a darker body. Lives in moist forest soils of Guyana, Suriname and French Guiana.

children.name: Microcaecilia grandis
children.description: Despite the genus name suggesting small size, this species is one of the larger Microcaecilia, found in northern Brazil and Suriname. Inhabits the leaf litter and upper soils of lowland tropical forest.

children.name: Microcaecilia iwokramae
children.description: A caecilian described from the Iwokrama rainforest of Guyana, one of the most biodiverse protected areas in South America. A tiny soil-dweller known from few specimens.

children.name: Microcaecilia marvaleejoneseae
children.description: Named after herpetologist Marvalee Wake, a leading authority on caecilian biology, this small Amazonian caecilian honors her lifetime contributions to amphibian research.

children.name: Microcaecilia rabei
children.description: A small caecilian from French Guiana named after a local naturalist. Burrows in the moist forest soils of the Guiana Shield lowlands.

children.name: Microcaecilia supernumeraria
children.description: Named for its supernumerary (extra) secondary annuli compared to related species, this small caecilian is found in the lowland forests of Suriname and northern Brazil.

children.name: Microcaecilia taylori
children.description: Named after the herpetologist Edward H. Taylor, this small caecilian of the Guianas is a soil-burrowing species of humid forest. Known from limited specimens.

children.name: Microcaecilia uyoyoensis
children.description: A caecilian from the Uyoyo area of Suriname, a recently described species from the lowland Amazonian forests. One of the smaller members of an already small genus.

children.name: Microcaecilia trombetas
children.description: A small caecilian discovered along the Trombetas River of the Brazilian Amazon, inhabiting the moist soils of terra firme forest. One of several Microcaecilia species described from Amazonian river systems.

children.name: Microcaecilia dermatostricta
children.description: A small caecilian of the northern Amazon basin named for distinctive tight-fitting skin around its annuli. Inhabits the deep soils of humid tropical forest.

children.name: Oscaecilia
children.description: Slender Neotropical caecilians with reduced eyes covered by bone, highly specialized soil burrowers of Central and South America. Many species have eyes completely fused beneath the skull.

children.name: Oscaecilia ochrocephala
children.description: A slender, pale-headed caecilian of Panama and Colombia with eyes covered by bone, an adaptation to a wholly subterranean life. Lays eggs in moist soil.

children.name: Oscaecilia bassleri
children.description: A slender burrowing caecilian of the Peruvian and Ecuadorian Amazon, named after the naturalist Harvey Bassler. Has eyes fused beneath the skull and inhabits lowland rainforest soils.

children.name: Oscaecilia elongata
children.description: One of the more elongate members of the genus, found in humid forests of Colombia and Ecuador. Its extremely slender body facilitates movement through compacted tropical soils.

children.name: Oscaecilia equatorialis
children.description: A caecilian of the equatorial forests of Ecuador and Peru, a soil-burrowing species with greatly reduced eyes. Rarely encountered despite inhabiting a biologically rich region.

children.name: Oscaecilia hypereumeces
children.description: A notably long, slender caecilian of the Amazon basin named for its unusually elongated body proportions. Burrows through the dense tropical soils of Peru and Brazil.

children.name: Oscaecilia koehleri
children.description: A burrowing caecilian of the Amazon lowlands named after the collector Koehler. Has bone-covered eyes and inhabits moist, well-drained forest soils.

children.name: Oscaecilia osae
children.description: A caecilian of the Osa Peninsula of Costa Rica, one of the most biodiverse areas of Central America. A slender burrowing species with reduced eyes, rarely encountered in the field.

children.name: Oscaecilia polyzona
children.description: A caecilian with numerous secondary annuli inhabiting the lowland forests of Colombia and Venezuela. A subterranean species with bone-covered eyes and a very slender body.

children.name: Oscaecilia zweifeli
children.description: Named after the herpetologist Richard G. Zweifel, this slender caecilian inhabits the tropical soils of northern South America. Known from scattered specimens collected in humid lowland forest.

children.name: Oscaecilia nicefori
children.description: A caecilian of Colombia named after Hermano Niceforo María, a naturalist who made important collections in Colombian herpetology. An elusive soil-burrower of Andean foothills.

children.name: Brasilotyphlus
children.description: A small genus of Neotropical caecilians endemic to Brazil, closely related to Caecilia but distinguished by cranial and annulation features. Found in the Amazon and Atlantic Forest regions.

children.name: Brasilotyphlus braziliensis
children.description: The type species of Brasilotyphlus, a medium-sized burrowing caecilian of the Amazon basin of Brazil. Lives in moist alluvial soils and is distinguishable from Caecilia by skull proportions and annulation.

children.name: Brasilotyphlus guarantanus
children.description: A caecilian of the Brazilian Amazon basin named after the Guaranta region. A secretive soil-burrower known from few specimens, inhabiting humid forest soils.

children.name: Luetkenotyphlus
children.description: A monotypic genus of Brazilian caecilians, named in honor of the zoologist Christian Friedrich Lütken. Closely related to Siphonops, with similar annulation and body form.

children.name: Luetkenotyphlus brasiliensis
children.description: The sole species of its genus, this caecilian of southeastern Brazil inhabits Atlantic Forest soils. Named after the Danish zoologist Christian Friedrich Lütken, it is a secretive burrower known from few localities.

children.name: Parvicaecilia
children.description: Small caecilians of northern South America and Panama, distinguished from other Neotropical caecilians by reduced secondary annuli and skull features. Among the least-studied caecilian genera.

children.name: Parvicaecilia pricei
children.description: A small caecilian of Colombia named after the collector Price. Inhabits moist soils of lowland forests and is rarely encountered; known from only a handful of museum specimens.

children.name: Parvicaecilia nicefori
children.description: A small Colombian caecilian named after Hermano Niceforo María, a prolific naturalist based in Medellín. Burrows in moist Andean foothill forest soils.

children.name: Parvicaecilia marthae
children.description: A small caecilian of northern Colombia, named after Martha, in whose honor it was described. A soil burrower of humid lowland and foothill forests.

children.name: Parvicaecilia toplainensis
children.description: A caecilian of the Topland region on the Colombia–Panama border, inhabiting montane forest soils at higher elevations than most Neotropical caecilians. A poorly known burrowing species.

children.name: Praslinia
children.description: A monotypic caecilian genus endemic to Praslin Island in the Seychelles, part of the remarkable relict caecilian radiation on the granitic Seychelles islands.

children.name: Praslinia cooperi
children.description: The only caecilian endemic to Praslin Island in the Seychelles, this species is part of an ancient island radiation isolated for tens of millions of years. A small, egg-laying soil burrower of forest and plantation soils.

children.name: Sylvacaecilia
children.description: A monotypic genus of West African caecilians from Cameroon and adjacent areas, one of several poorly known African genera requiring further study.

children.name: Sylvacaecilia grandisonae
children.description: A caecilian of Cameroon named in honor of the herpetologist Allison Grandison. The sole member of its genus, it inhabits the moist forest soils of Central West Africa and is rarely encountered in the field.

children.name: Idiocranium
children.description: A monotypic genus of West African caecilians known from Cameroon, named for the distinctive cranial morphology of its sole species. An elusive forest soil-dweller.

children.name: Idiocranium russeli
children.description: The sole species of Idiocranium, endemic to Cameroon and named after Russell. Notable for a uniquely shaped skull among African caecilians. An egg-laying, soil-burrowing species of humid montane forest.

children.name: Crotaphatrema
children.description: A small genus of Central African caecilians known from Cameroon, named for distinctive temporal openings in their skulls. Among the most poorly known caecilian genera.

children.name: Crotaphatrema bornmuelleri
children.description: A caecilian of Cameroon's highland forests named after the botanist Joseph Friedrich Nicolaus Bornmüller. Inhabits cool montane soils at elevations above 1000 m and is extremely rarely encountered.

children.name: Crotaphatrema lamottei
children.description: A caecilian of the Cameroon highlands named after the naturalist Maxime Lamotte who contributed to West African biodiversity studies. An elusive montane forest burrower known from very few specimens.

children.name: Crotaphatrema tchabalmbaboensis
children.description: Named after the Tchabal Mbabo massif in Cameroon where it was discovered, this caecilian inhabits high-altitude forest soils. One of the least-known caecilian species, described from only a few specimens.
